text: There is somthing very interesting happening in Australia with solar, panels are going down and old panels you can somtimes get for free or 10 -$50 . . . BUT feed in tarifs have gone down and peak tarifs and measuring tarifs have gone up. . . . Sooo our electricity providers are inadvertanly pushing consumers to do 1 or both of these things and this is where it gets interesting.🤩1 Self consumtion inverters to avoid measuring tarifs . . . . 🤩2 split morning and afternoon arrays/inverters to feed in at higher consumption /price times. Im trialing this now and so far its been a great way to save money for the interim until battery storage becomes more affordable.😊🌈

author: @anthonycali6880
text:
Thanks so much for this info! I've always been worried about cost of recycling. Only thing is, I don't know or believe when people sign these 25year contracts, if recycling is a part of this. I purchased 4 -100W panels, mounted to pressure treated plywood. Using 2000W inverter, 2-100AH lithium batteries. And loving it! I can run my espresso machine 10 min, cook in toaster oven, use toaster, rice cooker, crock pot, or hot pot. And I can use as backup if storm is coming. 
Being crazy, I added a 500W wind turbine, which so far has been rather useless, hoping for better results in the fall. So wind turbine so far failure, only 20 ft up, should be like 60ft, but too difficult to to install. So much infrastructure, would never do it again. I live in Massachusetts near many homes. So, for me, solar panels are the way to go in this location. I enjoy watching with your many ideas, just trying to charge batteries when no sun.

author: @theTeslaFalcon
text:
As we saw in 2020, math can be used dishonestly.
While solar panel assembly is very automated, recycling is very manual. Thus recycling the panels (disassembling, extraction, prep for reuse, etc) is cost-prohibitive / more expensive than raw materials. While certain parts are easier to separate than others (glass covers, aluminum frames, etc), the solar cells themselves are the hardest part.

The "panic" is based on panel rating over x time. X million panels were sold in 2015. W a 10 year expectant lifespan, that means that all x million panels will need to get "recycled" in 2025.

Method #1: The rich sell their old panels to someone looking for a deal. The 250w panels are now only putting out 200w, but that's ok. The rich buy new 500w panels & are happy. The cheapskate gets 200w panels to get into solar for the 1st time / add to an existing system.

Method #2: Recycle what's cheap & landfill the cells themselves since they aren't worth it. From the ground, back into the ground.

author: @MinkieWinkle
text:
the problem isn't that they can not be recycled. it is that it is cheaper to make new ones, no one, absolutely no one will be willingly to spend more to recycle a panel,  than what they will get back for actually doing so. E.g lets say it costs 10 a panels, but you only to get 5 back. the fact is, soon enough you will go broke, as you will. with every panel recycled you will be ever increasingly out of pocket. further into debt, until it all comes crashing down.

Firms that are mandated to recycle the panels only end up increasing the cost at which they sell new panels to off set the losses made when they recycle the old ones. so the consumer pays more.

Its like polystyrene, we always could recycle it for decades we have known how to do it. it is just isn't cost effective to do so. so we don't, we could break it all down in acetone. but again, it costs so much to do so. it simply is not viable to do so. 

I can hear to now "ITS NOT ABOUT MAKING MONEY" the fact is, it is. and always will be. the fact is, if you want it to be done, it has to be worth doing, else no one will be able to do it. it would be like working to get paid 100 pound a day, only to spend 120 pound a day traveling to work to earn that 100 pound. it is pointless. you are no better off, in fact you are worse off. so therefore you would not do it at all.

text: Again, Money is a big problem here. Companies don't want to resell used panels even if that got them for free. Some electricians won't install them as they can't be warrantied and in some places can't be insured. Customers want to upgrade because the new systems can produce far more power than their existing system can even though it still has 25 to 30 years left in it. so the full potential of old PV is wasted. It becomes the same as the IT industry and E-waste. Even in some places it is illegal to sell recycling to and end user. I can sell my scrap metal to the local recycling yard but I can't buy copper buss bars from them due to government regulation. While I could have reused it with little to no energy, now it has to go back to a smelter, a foundry and then to a factory to make it into a buss bar again.

author: @bootsowen
text:
I wonder if those recycling rules are a bit of a ruse to make more panels. If all of the removed panels have to be recycled then there is a much smaller used resale market for people like me. 

I’m glad you said that the recycling at the moment is 80% reuse. I bought a used set of panels a year ago and they are great. But when recycling means crushing everything up, recycling the easily recoverable metals and hiding the inert stuff like glass in road base then I don’t really see it as recycling.  

I have posted a few videos of my diy pv installation on my channel. Thanks

replies.author: @AnalystPrime
replies.text:
No, the recycling law is that if you sell me any electronics at all you pay for the recycling up front so I, or whoever I gave/sold it, can return the old device to your shop or otherwise drop it off for free. This means far less devices potentially containing hazardous materials are dumped by people who don't want to pay for proper disposal.
That also means anyone wanting to collect electronics for reuse can dispose of the stuff that does not work for free, so there is no hidden cost.

replies.author: @bootsowen
replies.text: @AnalystPrime I am pretty sure that the rules are different in different countries

author: @philchurch927
text: I understood that some of the componants in solar panels were highly toxic, is that true?

replies.author: @AnalystPrime
replies.text:
Yes, but the "environmentalists" warning about it are either idiots or paid shills.

PV panel is 1% "other metals", composition of which varies depending on what technology is used. Many of these metals are considered toxic, like copper, but one usually mentioned is the arsenic in gallium arsenide panels, which is silly because those are so expensive they are generally only used in satellites and more likely source of arsenic waste would be all the LEDs using it. Also, even when GaAs panels are used on Earth, the tiny amount of toxic material is still encased in glass. Solar panels might be out in weather for years or decades, but the stained glass windows in churches can be centuries old. Have you ever seen any church windows where the colors leaked out?

More likely problem could be cadmium in cadmium telluride thin film panels, as according to Wikipedia in 2011 they made up about 8% of panels, but those are used by big companies that would lose more money if they threw toxic waste in landfills rather than recycling it properly.

This is basically same issue as windmill blades. While the making of fiberglass involves some nasty chemicals and you don't want to breathe in any fibers from damaged fiberglass, the stuff is commonly used in items like boat hulls and water tanks, I even recall mention of fiberglass plates to eat from. Anyone honestly worried about windmills would have first focused on all the "toxic waste" created by boat owning millionaires and how it is used to store drinking water...

Great video Robert

replies.author: @yogawithrosie3805
replies.text: Why does the battery need replacing after 7 years.  It just has the same issue of degradation of around 1% per annum.  So just add in another battery to the array or live with 90% capacity.

replies.author: @FasterLower
replies.text:
Depening on usage Li-Ion batteries can last much more than 7 years. Tesla warranty for their powerwall is 10 years with a "100% depth of discharge" (DoD). The DoD is critical as the batteries life is mainly consumed at the top and bottom 20% of the charge range. Some systems artificuially constrict the operating range of the battery to maximise its life, others maximise usable capability today and sacrifice longevity. I looked at this a few years ago and I found that using 75% DoD a domestic system could easily last 15 years before reaching 90% capability. Of course while this is its "life" it is still has 90% of its original capability!

In the UK Nissan are now selling refurbished (thing rebadge the battery capability at 90% of original) batteries for home energy storage. So still plenty of usable life in "life expired" batteries. SAlways assuming that they were well designed in the first place!

replies.author: @rayg436
replies.text: @FasterLower ok I  see your point on the battery bank.  But the higher quality of battery the higher the price tag for the initial installation that most of us can't afford.

author: @DanielSMatthews
text: And then there is reality, the chances of panels lasting more than 20 years in some locations is very low due to all of the possible ways they can be damaged or degraded. Perhaps in the UK where even a nudist has a hard time getting a tan they will not degrade fast but under the Queensland sun they are noticeably degrading the moment they are installed. We also have nasty hail storms in most of our urbanised areas too so we regularly see huge swaths of destruction across entire suburbs where 100% of panels are pulverised by falling ice. 37% of Queensland households have solar on their roof and Australia has the highest uptake of solar globally, therefore it is that level of cover that ensures that they are constantly being trashed by nature. Then there is the fact that people will take them down half way through their "life" because the install was scaled on the false claim about their output, i.e. they do degrade fast enough for people to notice and want to replace them, I watched it happening on my neighbours place a few years back. They simply wanted more panels and more output from each panel and matching the old ones to the new ones is pesky enough for them to just trash the old ones.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I am sorry mate I have to disagree - you might want to read some of the comments from your fellow countrymen which is what I base my ideas on - even in Oz you are trashing too early

replies.author: @DanielSMatthews
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Yes, but humans are part of the formula, ignore the real world and it all looks different, but I am pointing out what happens when you do put actual human behavior into the picture. We don't make any PV silicon here on this continent (outside of research work), it would have to all be shipped back to the EU , USA  or China to be recycled fully. I know that sounds crazy but that is the situation here, and it is criminal, we should have a closed, circular, renewables economy, not waste so much energy moving them and their materials back and forth around the world.

author: @islandsedition
text: I looked into recycling a solar panel when I found a load at our local tip. The main issue I found was the resin/glue which bonded (I mean really really bonded) the glass to the panels. I forget the name of the stuff but I found the only solvent available was really hazardous to use and expensive. It led me to believe this was the main difficulty in recycling panels. Both the cost effectiveness and the hazardous chemicals needed.

replies.author: @thenaylor
replies.text: Maybe you could try heating it up instead of using solvents?

replies.author: @islandsedition
replies.text:
@thenaylor just re-familiarised myself with the problem. The adhesive layer is ethylene vinyl acetate. It has a softening point somewhere around 45'C and melts around 71'C.

Solar cells can only take up to about 65'C (apparently). So I guess yes, but you'd have to be able to controll your temperature either in a bath or oven. Really big ones! Especially if you're trying to. Preserve the cells.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: don't they use heat mostly?

replies.author: @islandsedition
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering from what I've seen you're right. But the companies I've looked at it seems they are focused on reclaiming the glass, then crush the cells into a dust for further reclamation of materials. I'm not sure heat is (commercially) used in a way to preserve and facilitate reuse of the cells.

author: @azlandpilotcar4450
text:
I don't think that I should say that it can't be done, but it is expensive.  Eu170 Bn  is a cost, not recovered value. The cost of replacement and recycling will be reflected in future increasing costs for deployment of new panels.  Scaled up for a mega scale generation facility, replacement and recycling would be a full-time operation, but an expense, not generation of revenue.

Imagine the cost 40 times greater when EU meets its short term goals, then thousands of times greater when more PV is deployed.   
Second hand PV sounds good, but places a burden on the handlers and the buyers.  Recycling glass is done usually with natural gas.  Extracting metals from cells and ICs is done with a combination of heat, chemical processes (that you are much more familiar with than I ) that are supposed to be similar to those used in refining ore, and electrical energy, few if any of which are provided without fossil fuels.
Panel replacement of a facility should occur when the cost of replacement is less than the "loss" of generating capability.  At today's prices, this is working out to be around 20 years, as you say, when the space allotted will be better used by new panels.  If they were more modular, and just a little "smarter", just the lowest output cells could be replaced -- like replacing a burned out bulb, rather than all the lighting fixtures.  I expect the service life will increase as the expense goes up, but this will be reflected in reduced output and greater overbuild "footprint" -- already a sticky point.  Best to plan on an initial 25% overbuild (unless some smart people can come up with ways of getting more energy -- through thermal recovery, for instance -- from the panels).
PV cost today is misleading.  Some countries are using low operating expenses, little or no pollution standards or enforcement, cheap tranportation, and often slave labor to deliberately undercut other suppliers.  This is obviously intended to set them as a real monopoly supplier, and if successful
Mostly I think of PV recycling in the same way as glass recycling, and much of PV is silicon and glass.  My community has just ended normal glass collection for recycling after supporting -- and legally requiring -- it for forty years.  The cost made it uneconomical to continue. They were paying by the ton for having the material shipped to where it could be recovered, and much of that material was more expansive and less desirable than raw materials for glass manufacturers.  Much of that stock has overgrown the storage facilities or been dumped. As of now, the local government is struggling to find a way to collect glass and do something with it, even though the local state university has donated patented processes to turn waste glass into useful products, it will require lots of money to actually set up the factories to make them -- much cheaper to scrap it in the local dump, as is being done now while the government dithers.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: ? sorry mate I quoted E2.7bn and that was taken from an industry report as value from recycling - in the US it costs somewhere between US$10-20 to recycle a panel - that doesn't seem that expensive to me - and recycling is more than just cost - how long can we just keep on consuming and dumping? in my opinion in the end that attitude is going to cost us more - well not me - I'll be dead - but us as in the human race

replies.text: I haven't mate but I am aware of them - perhaps I should

author: @evaemma5962
text: Why is it that my solar cell can not power my dc motor. But when i charge a capacity with it, and discharge it by connecting it with my dc motor. It rotate for a few period of time.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: your load is greater than your supply - think of it like a tap trying to turn a water wheel - if the tap can't supply enough water to push the wheel around - noting will happen -but if you fill a bath first then dump that on the wheel it will turn a bit - a capacitor is like a bath

author: @newrenewableenergycontrol5724
text:
Great report Bob! Sometimes I have a problem articulating this point in a discussion about solar panel viability! The 'can't be recycled' myth is one of the most common misunderstood points due to people trying to disprove solar. (Big Oil hates solar science. Eats into their profits massively) 
I had done what everyone does. Pronounce death to a 30 year old solar panel, which is not the truth. A solar panel half price of a new, that outputs 90% of its original energy is an excellent scenario for small applications. Or, better yet, a full scale installation could simply add 10% more panels to a working site to remain 100% the original install. A very large savings by pulling maximum working life out of an original installation.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: yeah absolutely it's that use it and throw it away mentality that I don't understand especially when there is plenty of life left in it - I am glad you enjoyed the video - cheers

author: @robtitheridge9708
text: Veolia, which runs the world’s only commercial-scale silicon PV recycling plant in France, shreds and grinds up panels and then uses an optical technique to recover low-purity silicon. According to Vanderhoof, Recycle PV Solar initially used a “heat process and a ball mill process” that could recapture more than 90 percent of the materials present in a panel, including low-purity silver and silicon. But the company recently received some new equipment from its European partners that can do “95 plus percent recapture,” he said, while separating the recaptured materials much better.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I believe they launched in 2017 - but isn't there a new one Rosi solar now?

author: @LoremIpsum1970
text: It's all down to legislation. So, is this a debunk or confirmation of Undecided with Matt Ferrell -- The Truth About Solar Panels?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: neither really - he is only really talking about the state of recycling in the USA - which - though I hate to say it is behind Europe - but they will catch up when they can see a profit can be made lol - I hadn't seen it until you mentioned it so I went and looked - there are a couple of - not exactly errors - but points he fudges to make his point - but I am ok with that

author: @rtfazeberdee3519
text: Plenty of myths from the naysayers - another one is that they leach toxic chemicals

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I think that is down to matt fares as he talks about lead and cadmium - there is lead in silicon pv as it used in the solder - but far less than say in a TV set - there is no cadmium in silicon solar cells but it is present in cadmium Telluride cells - but as 90% of solar cells are of the silicon type - it's a bit of a fudge

author: @jezlawrence720
text:
100% diversity of energy production and storage is the answer. 

Solar is vital.

But so is wind, tidal, batteries, hydro, gravity and nuclear.

Most of that can be done with material which can be recycled, and many of them can or do use recycled raw materials to begin with e.g. waste rubble in a gravity or heat battery

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I agree mate

author: @no1be4me2
text: Also I'm wondering the energy it takes to make this stuff like the solar cells and wind generatorswhen we recycle it takes more energy to recycle so I'm figuring then that's a loss of energy someone comment the steel could be recycled but if you have to melt it down to use it for other stuff that's going to take energy so the total output we have the minus that I know there's still a plus but not as much

replies.author: @brandonboulton2776
replies.text: Sometimes it's more about the cost to mine it fiscally and ecologically vs. the cost to recycle. For instance it is abundantly more fiscally and ecologically responsible to recycle aluminum as apposed to continually mining, refining, etc ...

If you are starting from a broken panel _all the mining and refining are already done_

author: @matthewellisor5835
text:
I just bought more used panels.
250W rated, estimated 7-8 years old. They weren't permitted to tell me exactly but a few questions like what country, state, county... and 15 minutes of digging around narrowed it to 1 of 2 in Florida. Well, how about I go look up when they were built.
Anyway, I got them landed on two skids for less than $70ea and the shipping was a substantial portion of that.
I'd expect that they'll provide 180W for most of my 5 average hours a day.

replies.text: nice - cheers mate
